prompt: if i run ./entire search then I see this error Error: parsing response: invalid character '<' looking for beginning of value but if I run ENTIRE_API_BASE_URL=http://localhost:8787 ENTIRE_SEARCH_URL=http://localhost:8686 ./entire search then no errors. whats going on
answer:
Now I have the full picture. Here's exactly what's happening:

---

## Root Cause: Two separate services, both defaulting to `https://entire.io`

Without the env vars, **both** `ENTIRE_API_BASE_URL` and `ENTIRE_SEARCH_URL` default to the production URL `https://entire.io`. The search command hits `https://entire.io/search/v1/search`, which in your local dev environment is either unreachable or returns an **HTML error page** (e.g. a 404, redirect, or Cloudflare error page) instead of JSON.

When `json.Unmarshal` tries to parse that HTML body, it immediately chokes on the `<` in `<!DOCTYPE html>` or `<html>`, producing the error:

```
Error: parsing response: invalid character '<' looking for beginning of value
```

Here's the exact line in `search/search.go:302-303` where this happens:

```go
var result Response
if err := json.Unmarshal(body, &result); err != nil {
    return nil, fmt.Errorf("unexpected response from search service: %s", string(body))
}
```

> Note: the error message in the code actually says `"unexpected response from search service"`, so the `"parsing response"` prefix you see is likely wrapped further up the call stack in `search_cmd.go:154-155`:
> ```go
> resp, err := search.Search(ctx, searchCfg)
> if err != nil {
>     return fmt.Errorf("search failed: %w", err)
> }
> ```

---

## Why setting both env vars fixes it

| Env Var | Default (broken) | With env var |
|---|---|---|
| `ENTIRE_API_BASE_URL` | `https://entire.io` | `http://localhost:8787` (your local API) |
| `ENTIRE_SEARCH_URL` | `https://entire.io` | `http://localhost:8686` (your local search service) |

When you set `ENTIRE_SEARCH_URL=http://localhost:8686`, the search request goes to your **locally running search service** at `localhost:8686/search/v1/search`, which responds with actual JSON — so parsing succeeds.

---

## The fix

The error message is also misleading. Instead of silently swallowing the JSON parse error and only printing the body (which may be a huge HTML blob), `search.go:302-303` could give a much clearer error. Consider changing it to something like:

```go
if err := json.Unmarshal(body, &result); err != nil {
    return nil, fmt.Errorf("parsing response (status %d, body: %.200s): %w", resp.StatusCode, string(body), err)
}
```

This would surface both the HTTP status code and a snippet of the bad response body, making it immediately obvious that an HTML page was returned rather than JSON.
